summary: Mississippi was the central place where we organized voter registration drives during the early 1960s. It was a tough environment, full of fear and resistance, especially in counties like Pike, Amite, and Walthall. We faced intimidation and arrests, but we persisted in our efforts to empower African Americans to register to vote. The state's political and social climate made our work both dangerous and necessary.

Clovell, Mississippi was supposed to be the starting point for our voter registration drive in the Delta region. Unfortunately, we couldn't begin there because we lacked the necessary equipment. It was a planned location, but we had to shift our efforts elsewhere due to logistical issues.

McComb, Mississippi became the actual place where we launched our voter registration drive in the summer of 1961. At the invitation of O. C. Bryant, the local NAACP head, we canvassed door-to-door and engaged local leaders to support our efforts. McComb was also the site of the first sit-ins, including the Woolworth lunch counter protest, which stirred the town and energized our movement.

O. C. Bryant was the local head of the NAACP in McComb, Mississippi, who invited us to start the voter registration drive there. His support was crucial in setting up the project when we couldn't begin in Clovell. Bryant was a key figure in organizing and facilitating civil rights activities in McComb during 1961.

The NAACP played a vital role in supporting our voter registration efforts in Mississippi. They provided financial assistance for students' room, board, and transportation, and helped with bond money when I was jailed in Amite County. The organization was a backbone for civil rights activism, connecting local leaders like O. C. Bryant and helping us navigate legal challenges.

The Delta region in Mississippi was initially planned as the starting point for our voter registration drive. However, due to lack of equipment, we had to postpone and shift our efforts to McComb. The Delta represented a challenging area where civil rights work was desperately needed.

Magnolia, Mississippi is the county seat of Pike County, where people began to go to register to vote after our canvassing efforts. It was the official place for voter registration in Pike County, and we encouraged locals to come there despite the risks. Magnolia symbolized the administrative center where the struggle for voting rights took place.

Pike County was one of the first counties where we conducted voter registration drives starting in August 1961. We canvassed door-to-door in McComb and encouraged people to register in Magnolia, the county seat. Pike County was somewhat less hostile compared to neighboring counties, but it was still a difficult environment for civil rights work.

Amite County was one of the tougher counties adjacent to Pike County where we extended our voter registration efforts. We made several attempts to register voters in Liberty, the county seat, facing intimidation and violence, including my arrest and beating. Despite the hostility, we set up schools at Steptoe's house to educate local farmers and continued pushing for registration.

Walthall County was another adjacent county to Pike where we sent students to begin voter registration work around mid-August 1961. The local people were eager to participate, and we helped conduct schools to prepare them for registration attempts. Walthall represented part of the expanding civil rights efforts in the region despite the risks involved.

Liberty, Mississippi, the county seat of Amite County, was a focal point for our voter registration attempts in August 1961. We faced intense scrutiny and intimidation from local officials and law enforcement, including a Highway Patrolman who followed and arrested me. Despite the fear, we managed to get people to fill out registration forms and continued efforts there after violent incidents.

The Highway Patrolman, particularly Officer Carlisle, played a hostile role during our voter registration efforts in Amite County. Carlisle tailed us for miles, stopped our car, and physically pushed me while using racial slurs. His presence was part of the intimidation tactics used by law enforcement to discourage civil rights activities in 1961 Mississippi.

Carlisle was the Highway Patrolman who aggressively followed and stopped us after a voter registration attempt in Liberty, Amite County. He pushed me out of the car using racial slurs and arrested me on dubious charges of interfering with an officer. This encounter led to my first experience in a Mississippi jail and highlighted the dangers we faced from law enforcement.

The United States Justice Department was a critical ally during our voter registration campaign in Mississippi. When I was arrested, I contacted them to explain the protections under the Civil Rights Acts of 1957 and 1960. They advised us to call them or the FBI if we encountered trouble, providing some legal backing against local harassment.

The Civil Rights Act of 1957 was one of the federal laws that guaranteed protection to people trying to register to vote in Mississippi. I referenced this act when I was arrested to assert our legal rights and to seek assistance from the Justice Department. It was a vital piece of legislation that supported our efforts against local suppression.

The Civil Rights Act of 1960 further strengthened protections for voter registration activities, which we relied on during our campaign in Mississippi. When I was arrested, I cited this act to the Justice Department to ensure we had federal support. This law helped provide a legal framework to challenge local intimidation and obstruction.

The FBI was another federal agency we could contact for help if we faced trouble during voter registration efforts. After my arrest in Amite County, I called the FBI's civil rights office in Washington, D.C., to report the intimidation we experienced. Their involvement was part of the federal oversight intended to protect civil rights workers.

The Amite County Jail was where I spent a couple of days after being arrested during a voter registration attempt in 1961. It was my first experience with Mississippi jails, and I was only released after bond money came through via the NAACP. The jail symbolized the physical risks civil rights activists faced in the state.

Steptoe was the local NAACP president in southern Amite County who hosted voter education schools at his house. We taught farmers there in late August 1961, despite transportation challenges and the scattered nature of the farms. Steptoe's support was crucial in extending our outreach into rural areas of Amite County.

Woolworth in McComb was the site of the first sit-in in the town during August 1961. Otowells and Curtis Dawson sat in at the Woolworth lunch counter, sparking a big uproar and energizing the civil rights movement locally. This event coincided with our voter registration efforts and helped galvanize community activism.

The McComb sit-ins began in August 1961 with local activists like Otowells and Curtis Dawson challenging segregation at lunch counters. These protests created a stir in the town and were part of a broader strategy to fight racial injustice alongside voter registration drives. The sit-ins energized the community and led to mass meetings and further activism.

Billy Jack was one of the boys who confronted us after I was severely beaten during a voter registration attempt in Liberty, Amite County. His group accosted us as we tried to leave town, adding to the intimidation and violence we faced. This incident underscored the physical dangers civil rights workers encountered in Mississippi.

Curtis Dawson was a fellow activist who worked closely with me during the voter registration drives and civil rights activities in Mississippi. He participated in the McComb sit-ins and was with me during the violent registration attempt in Liberty in August 1961. Dawson was a committed leader in the movement, helping to organize and sustain local efforts.

Preacher Martin joined Curtis Dawson and me on a voter registration attempt in Liberty, Amite County, on August 22, 1961. We faced severe violence that day, and I was badly beaten. Despite the attack, Martin remained part of our efforts to encourage voter registration and civil rights activism in the area.
